title: SEGMENT 12 — Looking at living cells: confocal, two-photon, light-sheet

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Looking at living cells: confocal, two-photon, light-sheet
So you have all these beautiful fluorescent proteins and sensors.
Now we need to talk about how you actually image them, because the choice of microscope matters enormously.
A bad microscope can wreck a good fluorescent-protein experiment.
Let me walk you through the main options.
Widefield epifluorescence microscopy.
This is the basic fluorescence microscope.
You illuminate the entire field of view from above through the objective lens, you collect the emitted fluorescence with a camera, and that's it.
It's fast, simple, sensitive.
The downside: you excite light everywhere in the depth of the sample, so light from above and below your focal plane all contributes as out-of-focus blur.
For thin samples — cultured cells in a dish — widefield is often the right answer because it's gentle and fast.
For thick samples — tissue slices, embryos — the blur becomes intolerable.
Confocal microscopy.
The analogy here is helpful: think of confocal as taking a clean optical slice through your sample, like a CT scanner but for visible light.
Instead of imaging the whole depth, you image just one thin focal plane, sharply.
How does it do that?
With a clever optical trick: a tiny pinhole sitting in the detection path that physically blocks out-of-focus light.
You scan a focused laser spot across the sample, one point at a time, and at each point you collect only the light coming from exactly that focal point.
Light from above or below the focal plane misses the pinhole and is discarded.
The result is a sharp optical section through your sample at one depth.
By moving the focus up or down and scanning again, you can build up a stack of slices and reconstruct a three-dimensional image — exactly like a CT scan, but with light instead of x-rays.
Confocal is the workhorse of cellular imaging for both fixed and live samples.
Trade-offs: it's slower than widefield because you have to scan point by point, and it dumps a lot of light into the sample, which is harsh on live cells and accelerates photobleaching.
Spinning-disk confocal is a clever speed solution.
Instead of one laser spot scanning serially, a spinning disk full of pinholes illuminates and detects many points simultaneously, producing a confocal image in real time, at video rates.
Spinning-disk is faster and gentler than point-scanning confocal, and it's often the preferred choice for live-cell time-lapse imaging where you need optical sectioning at speed.
Two-photon microscopy.
Here the analogy is more elaborate, but it's worth it.
Imagine that normally, to excite your fluorophore, you need one big photon — say, a green photon.

In two-photon excitation, you instead use two smaller, lower-energy photons — say, two red photons — that have to arrive at the same molecule at almost the same instant, within a femtosecond of each other.
Only then does the combined energy of the two-photon collision excite the fluorophore.
Why is that useful?
Because the probability of two photons arriving at the same place at the same instant scales as the square of the local light intensity.
So the excitation only happens at the very tightest spot of a focused laser beam, where the intensity is maximal.
Even a tiny bit above or below the focal point, the intensity drops and the rate of two-photon collisions falls off a cliff.
You get inherent optical sectioning — only the focal point gets excited — without any need for a pinhole.
There are two big bonuses.
First, because you're using longer-wavelength light, typically near-infrared around 900 to 1000 nanometers, it scatters less in tissue.
You can image hundreds of micrometers deep into a brain.
Second, because nothing outside the focal point gets excited, nothing outside the focal point bleaches.
Your surrounding tissue stays fresh and unharmed even as you scan repeatedly through a particular plane.
Two-photon microscopy has become the dominant method for imaging GCaMP in awake-behaving mice.
The catch is cost: it requires a very expensive pulsed femtosecond laser, and the imaging is point-scanning, so it's slow.
Light-sheet microscopy.

A different approach to optical sectioning entirely, and the analogy is direct: shine a thin sheet of light sideways through the sample, illuminating only the layer you're currently imaging, like cutting the sample with a laser knife.
The detection objective looks down at the illuminated plane from above.
Nothing above or below that plane is illuminated, so nothing else bleaches or contributes to the image.
The results are beautiful.
Gorgeous optical sectioning, very low light dose to the sample as a whole, very fast imaging — you can capture full 3D volumes in seconds.
Light-sheet has become the method of choice for imaging large, fragile specimens — developing embryos, organoids, transparent zebrafish larvae — over long periods without phototoxicity.
The downside: the geometry requires specialized sample mounting, and the equipment is sophisticated.
A few practical notes that tie back to fluorescent proteins.
Different modalities place different demands on the FP.
Two-photon imaging requires proteins that absorb well at the long wavelengths the laser uses — not all FPs are equally good two-photon absorbers.
Long time-lapse confocal or two-photon stresses photostability above all.
Light-sheet is gentle enough that almost any FP works.
The general rule: match the microscope to the biological question first, then choose the FP that performs best under that microscope's specific demands.
Don't pick an FP in the abstract and try to image it however you can.
Plan the imaging pipeline as one integrated system from the start.
And remember the trade-off that runs through all of this: brighter and longer imaging means more photons, more photons means more photobleaching and more phototoxicity.
Every photon you collect is a small insult to the cell.
The art of live-cell imaging is collecting just enough photons to answer your question without dosing the cell into damage.
Understanding how the FP works, how it bleaches, and what the microscope is doing to it is what lets you walk that line.
